data.title: Phillips 66: The Trajectory Is Improving
data.description:
Shares of Phillips 66 (NYSE:PSX) have been a poor performer over the past year as the company has dealt with a difficult refining cycle and challenges in its chemicals unit. This has left shares down 10% from a year ago. Indeed, performance has been so disappointing that Elliott managed to win two board seats in its proxy fight. Given Elliott's success with streamlining Marathon Petroleum (MPC), I am optimistic this can be a constructive engagement. I last covered shares in May, rating PSX a "buy," and since then, the stock has returned 15%, broadly in-line with the market. With updated financials and several actions, now is a good time to revisit shares.

Phillips 66 has a highly-integrated model. As you can see below, it owns refineries, significant pipeline assets, a marketing business, retail fuel operations in Europe, and a chemical joint venture with Chevron (CVX). This gives it more diverse cash flow than a firm like Marathon or Valero (VLO), but it also has been a reason why activists see room to sell assets and narrow the company's focus.

Frankly, it appears that PSX is getting the message to an extent. In May, Phillips 66 agreed to sell a 65% stake in its European retail business, resulting in pre-tax cash proceeds of $1.6 billion. This will help focus the company's operations and support debt reduction. On the earnings call, management also said there are no "sacred cows" in terms of divesting assets. In the medium term, I question the benefits of housing the chemicals JV inside of PSX. However given how weak the chemical market is and how badly those stocks have performed, now is unlikely to be an opportune time to sell the asset, especially given it has a favorable cost structure in the Middle East.

Rather, I expect an ongoing source of speculation to be whether PSX decides to spin out its midstream assets into a separate MLP, especially given how MPC has done that to great effect with MPLX (MPLX). This is not my base case, but it is a definite possibility. Having rolled in DCP Midstream and PSXP, PSX has gone the other direction to more clearly consolidate control. Thanks to these actions, its midstream unit has grown significantly, and there will likely be an examination of the best structure going forward.

In the company's second quarter, we did see meaningful improvement. Phillips 66 earned $2.38, which beat estimates by $0.67. Adjusted EBITDA tripled sequentially to $2.5 billion, thanks to a stronger refining environment after a dismal Q1. While PSX owns multiple businesses, refining is generally the unit that causes the most QoQ volatility, and it is still the core of the business. Importantly, results here were strong.

PSX's facilities ran at 98% utilization in Q2, which was the best quarterly result in over 6 years. PSX has been engaged in a multiyear effort of turnaround and maintenance activity to upgrade the quality of its refineries after a period of underinvestment, and we are starting to see the benefits of this spending with better throughput. Plus, operating costs per barrel hit a four-year low of $5.46/barrel. Guidance on full year turnaround cost was reduced by $100 million to $400-$450 million, another incremental positive.

Overall, refining generated a $392 million pre-tax profit from a $936 million loss a year ago. Its realized refining margin was $11.25, up 12% from last year and by nearly $5 from Q1. Refiners make money on the spread between products and crude oil, known as a crack spread. Last year, crack spreads narrowed meaningfully amid strong global refinery throughput. With OPEC+ increasing oil production and global growth remaining positive, we have seen crack spreads rebound meaningfully. I view current levels as more in keeping with normal crack spreads with about ~$2-3 of upside relative to Q2 levels. With where spreads are now, we should see a similar or slightly higher margin in the third quarter.

Elsewhere, midstream EBITDA was $972 million, up from $885 million in Q1. It completed its Coastal Bend acquisition during the quarter. This alongside organic expansion activity should propel midstream towards $4.5 billion of EBITDA by 2027. Chemicals earned just $20 million. Tariff disruptions were particularly problematic, leading to excess supply in the quarter, as exports to China were diverted. Fortunately, it is seeing chemical capacity rationalization in Europe. Lower supply should help to stabilize pricing and profits, but I expect the recovery here to be gradual with results running below mid-cycle norms at least into 2027.

During the quarter, PSX generated $845 million in cash from operations, despite $1 billion of working capital drains. With this, it did $419 million of buybacks, and as a result of these repurchases, there has been a 4% share count reduction over the past year. Alongside this, PSX pays a 4% dividend yield, which I view as very secure. That is because management has structured its dividend and sustaining cap-ex needs to be covered by less-volatile midstream cash flows. However, its balance sheet is not where I would like to see it.

The company's debt to capital is now 41% whereas it wants net debt to capital below 30% by 2027. Given how cyclical and volatile refining margins can be, entities like PSX should not have much financial leverage. While it has $1.1 billion of cash on hand, it has $20.9 billion of debt. For comparison, management reiterated its aim for $17 billion of consolidated debt. Now, PSX added $2.4 billion of debt during the quarter to fund $2.2 billion of acquisitions in midstream.

This is why I was encouraged to see the $1.6 billion in proceeds from its European retail unit sale, and this will go to fund most of that acquisition and shifting from European retail to American midstream assets is a winning asset transition in my view. I do expect the company to continue to seek divestiture opportunities to speed up its deleveraging. Absent that, we are likely to see PSX slow the pace of repurchases to restore balance sheet strength.

Back in May, I was targeting $2.5-$3.25 billion in 2025 free cash flow and this is with chemicals and refining running below normal this year. I still see the company generating about $3 billion of free cash flow, assuming the current refining environment persists and working capital normalizes. With Elliott's involvement, I believe PSX is well positioned to hit its financial targets and approach $7 billion in free cash flow in 2027. I was also encouraged by operational improvement this quarter. As such, I still see shares gradually rising towards $165 over the next 18 months with asset sales a potential positive catalyst. I continue to see PSX as a buy.

data.title: Vertex Inc.: Moving To The Sidelines As Near-Term Growth Expectations Get Reset
data.description:
Investment summary

My previous investment thought on Vertex Inc. (NASDAQ:VERX) was an upgrade to a buy rating because the growth drivers were intact andthe valuation was also at a more reasonable level. However, 2Q25 results have tempered my conviction in the near term. While cloud momentum and early e-invoicing traction remain strong, the slowdown in ERP migrations and softer customer expansion activity have meaningfully reset expectations. With FY25 guidance cut and growth reacceleration now less certain in the near term, I think sentiment will stay muted until there’s tangible proof of recovery. As such, I am moving to the sidelines.

2Q25 Earnings Review

2Q25 results did not live up to my expectations. Headline numbers were solid. Revenue grew 14.6% y/y to $184.6 million, with software subscriptions up 15.7% and services up 8.3%. Gross margin also expanded >200 bps y/y to 75.9%. But this strength didn’t flow through to earnings. Adj. EBITDA was flat y/y at $38.4M, with margin compressing to 20.8% from 23.9%, which reflects heavier investment spending. Most notably, a cut to FY25 guidance overshadowed the positives this quarter showed, and I think this has led to the market resetting near-term expectations (share price fell 18% post 2Q25 earnings).

Cloud Growth and E-Invoicing

VERX cloud momentum remains the crux of my long-term bullish thesis. Performance here is clearly strong, with revenue growing by 29.9% y/y to $86.2 million, and management reaffirmed its FY25 cloud growth target of 28%. This speaks very well of the underlying demand because total revenue guidance was trimmed. For investors, I believe this matters because it basically shows that the weakness is in the on-premise business, which is cyclical noise, while the cloud business continues to move in the right direction. As a percentage of total subscription sales, cloud now accounts for 55%, up from 49% a year ago. From this, it is apparent that the mix shift is happening.

Apart from the cloud, e-invoicing, which I’ve previously highlighted as a major long-term optionality, is also showing its first tangible sign of materialization. I got this inkling from the fact that Ecosio's annual recurring revenue [ARR] grew 33% sequentially to $10.8 million. This is a massive acceleration, clearly showing that VERX is benefiting from the strong deal flow ahead of upcoming European e-invoicing mandates. As a recap, Belgium’s deadline is set for January 2026, and as France and Germany’s deadlines get cast in stone, I expect more companies to rush into getting the e-invoicing set up and done to avoid compliance issues.

This dynamic goes beyond just VERX benefiting from a strong flow of deals; it also significantly extends the growth runway as there is a land-and-expand motion going on. Management gave a good example here:an early customer has already expanded to additional country coverage post-launch. If this trend continues, it should become a key driver of sustained net revenue retention [NRR] expansion.

Macro Headwinds and Lowered Expectations Reset the Clock

All the forward-looking growth angles are great, but I see a need to downgrade this to hold rating for the near term because of a few reasons. Firstly, the risk of a needed 2H25 reacceleration that I mentioned in my risk section previously has unfortunately materialized, but not in the way I would have hoped. It is evident from the 2Q25 results that the business is indeed being impacted by macro headwinds. You can see this from organic ARR growth slowing to 14.1% y/y from 15.1% in 1Q25, and NRR declining one point sequentially to 108%.

What is more worrisome is that management said there was a slowdown in large ERP migrations, which is elongating deal cycles. I see this as a very negative development because the ERP upgrade cycle was not just a minor tailwind; I actually saw this as one of the most visible and strong drivers of ARR growth for the next three years. This slowdown directly challenged my bull thesis. My idea was that because tax compliance is mission-critical and must be embedded into the core financial stack, through this ERP migration, VERX gets pulled into the migration timeline early, giving it a valuable "head start" to cross-sell its broader platform. With ERP projects now being delayed, VERX loses this crucial, early entry point, and the downstream impact is a delay in VERX sales pipeline. This creates a narrative problem as it impacts investor confidence and expectations given that the high-visibility, multi-year $1.8 billion TAM opportunity tied to these migrations now appears less certain in the near term. While the CEO noted this cycle has been slow in 2025 but expects it to accelerate eventually, I don’t think this explanation is enough to help investors regain confidence.

Additionally, there was a pronounced slowdown in customers’ own business growth. This is also problematic because this means that customers are keeping their existing contracted usage bands. In other words, there is reduced incremental revenue from extra usage that VERX can benefit from.

All in all, this drove management to guide down FY25 revenue, now at $750–$754 million, which I think has really hurt the stock sentiment. In my view, this revision will likely keep the stock range-bound until there is tangible evidence of a recovery in expansion activity and a clear path to reacceleration.

Valuation

The foreseeable near-term is going to be tough for VERX. The stock has generally trended in line with its growth expectations. From late FY22 to early FY25, the market has continuously expected better growth in this period, and that has driven valuation (forward revenue) from ~4x all the way up to ~11.5x. However, with the headwinds I mentioned, especially the delay in ERP migration, growth expectations have reset very strongly. Consequently, valuation has come down. The way I see it, VERX has to show that growth can reaccelerate; if not, the stock will be unlikely to see any meaningful valuation re-rating. I downgrade to hold.

Conclusion

I downgrade VERX to a Hold rating. The near-term picture has changed for the worse, with macro headwinds and ERP migration delays impacting growth. Until VERX can demonstrate momentum in ARR growth and expansion activity, I expect the stock to remain range-bound. In my view, investors are going to wait for a clearer reacceleration before revisiting the stock.

data.title: Wall Street Breakfast Podcast: UnitedHealth Jumps As Funds Boost Stakes
data.description:
Listen below or on the go on Apple Podcasts and Spotify

Berkshire Hathaway (BRK.B) takes new stakes in UnitedHealth (UNH), Nucor (NUE); pares back on Apple (AAPL). (00:22) Intel (INTC) spikes on speculation US considers taking stake in chip company: report. (01:22) Airbnb (ABNB) launches more flexible payment options with Reserve Now, Pay Later. (03:12)

This is an abridged transcript.

United Health is on the move this morning after 13F filings were disclosed.

Warren Buffett's Berkshire Hathaway (NYSE:BRK.B) (NYSE:BRK.A) disclosed that it acquired a new stake in the health insurer during Q2 2025. It also took new stakes in Nucor (NUE) and D.R. Horton (DHI).

Berkshire acquired 5.04M shares of UnitedHealth (UNH) valued at $1.57B.

Its Apple (AAPL) holdings declined, as did its stake in Bank of America (BAC).

Eminence Capital exited its stake in Bank of America (NYSE:BAC), Uber (NYSE:UBER), GE Healthcare (NASDAQ:GEHC), Capital One (COF) and Primo Brands (PRMB), while taking new positions in UnitedHealth (NYSE:UNH), Coupang (CPNG) and Peloton Interactive (NASDAQ:PTON), among other moves in the second quarter.

The global asset management firm disclosed new positions in UnitedHealth (NYSE:UNH) with 0.57M shares.

Renaissance Technologies is also betting on UnitedHealth Group (NYSE:UNH), taking a new stake of 1.35M shares.

Premarket United Health is up 13%.

Intel (NASDAQ:INTC) closed the day Thursday 7.38% higher after Bloomberg reported the Trump Administration is considering the U.S. taking a financial stake in the company.

The report said the plan would help Intel develop a factory hub in Ohio.

According to people familiar with the issue, At one point, Intel planned to develop the site into the largest chip foundry on the planet. There are no details on the amount the U.S. would invest in the company or even its legality as the plan remains "fluid."

"We look forward to continuing our work with the Trump administration to advance these shared priorities, but we are not going to comment on rumors or speculation," Intel said to Bloomberg.

The plan allegedly emerged from a meeting earlier this week between Intel CEO Lip-Bu Tan and U.S. President Donald Trump at the White House.

Airbnb is now offering more flexible payment options.

U.S. guests can now reserve an Airbnb (NASDAQ:ABNB) with no money down with the company’s “Reserve Now, Pay Later” option.

The new policy is available for listings with a moderate or flexible cancellation policy. It allows the guest up to the end of the listing’s free cancellation period to pay the full amount of the listing. Cancellation policies selected by hosts remain unchanged.

Airbnb (NASDAQ:ABNB) found that 60% of respondents to a recent survey said flexible payment options were important when making a reservation, while 55% said they use a flexible payment option when available. Additionally, 42% of respondents said they have delayed and missed out on their preferred accommodation option because of time spent coordinating payment with co-travelers.

The “Reserve Now, Pay Later” option is only available for U.S. guests booking eligible U.S.-based listings.

What’s Trending on Seeking Alpha:

Applied Materials signals Q4 revenue decline to $6.7B amid China uncertainty and leading-edge logic delays

Cathie Wood bets big on Bullish IPO, buys over 2.5M shares

Oracle to offer Google’s Gemini AI models to its customers

Dow, S&P and Nasdaq futures are in mixed territory after U.S. stocks finished Thursday's trading session mostly flat as hopes of progress on Russia and Ukraine countered a hit to sentiment from a significantly hotter than expected July PPI report. U.S. President Donald Trump will meet with Russian leader Vladimir Putin today in Alaska. By the way, that's the topic of today’s edition of the Wall Street Breakfast newsletter. Here’s a link to sign up.

Crude oil is down 0.9% at $61/barrel. Bitcoin is up 0.4% at $118,000. Gold is up 0.25% at $3,344.

The FTSE 100 is flat and the DAX is up 0.4%. And the market in India is closed today for a holiday.

The biggest movers for the day premarket: Applied Materials (NASDAQ:AMAT) -14% – Shares sank after the chip equipment maker topped FQ3 expectations but cut its Q4 outlook, citing macroeconomic and policy headwinds, including weaker demand from China.

On today’s economic calendar:

  • 8:30 am Retail Sales for the month of July. It will include the Amazon Prime Day event and corresponding sales events across the retail sector.

  • 9:15 am Industrial Production

data.title: Putting America First With First Solar
data.description:
I find it rather odd how climate change, environmental protection, and all of that dominate the discussion around solar panels. I believe humanity contributes to climate change, and I do believe in safeguarding the environment. Still, when I look at solar panels, the first thing I think of is “national security.” If the United States were to end up in a hot war with China, America’s energy infrastructure could quickly come under attack. While I find the prospect of a hot war with China low, as far as national security is concerned, we should plan for it as an inevitability, and among other things, that means strengthening our energy grid, and solar panels can contribute to that. Even outside of a hot war, cyber attacks by shadowy groups could knock out energy grids and energy infrastructure, including perhaps even oil pipelines and refineries.

(On a side note: there is some debate over how much solar panels will reduce humanity’s contribution to climate change, as producing the panels is energy-intensive, among other things. Additionally, solar panel production can damage the environment. Since I’m looking at panels from a national security POV, I am not as concerned about these factors, although important discussions should take place.)

Even outside of cyberattacks and wars, energy grids can be knocked out by storms and other events. Ultimately, strengthening and diversifying the energy grid could yield many benefits. Which is where First Solar (NASDAQ:FSLR) comes in. It is the largest producer of solar panels in the United States, producing 50% more panels than the next biggest American company. (The next biggest producer is Q-Cells, which is headquartered in South Korea but has production facilities in Georgia.)

There are many limitations to solar panels, of course. They only work in some climates and cloudy weather reduces energy production. They also only generate energy through part of the day. On its own, solar energy won’t be enough for most (if not all) energy grids and local demand markets. Still, they can reduce pressure on traditional energy grids, and in many cases, can provide energy even if the grid goes down, say due to a storm or cyberattack. That said, for grid-tied solar panels to provide energy when the grid goes down, they typically do need to be paired with a battery. Fortunately, battery storage costs have been declining over the years, making a battery setup more obtainable. Tariffs may send prices upward again, but still, historical trends suggest an overall decline.

Thesis

First Solar is an attractive investment opportunity with a fair valuation and good long-term prospects. President Donald Trump’s Big Beautiful Bill creates drawbacks, but also catalysts for growth, including protecting American solar manufacturers from dumping. China’s solar industry is a serious threat, but overcapacity and high debt burdens are pressuring Chinese firms.

Can America Challenge China’s Solar Domination?

China currently dominates the global solar market in all stages, which are illustrated above. China is also generating the most demand. Unfortunately, North America, and Europe as well, are relatively minor players. Other countries in the Asia Pacific (APAC) region have a larger presence in production, but are still relatively small players compared to China.

China’s efforts to dominate the solar panel industry have created an estimated 300,000 manufacturing jobs (ibid.). One might wonder if China has become overly reliant on manufacturing exports, as price wars and deflationary pressures mount, but regardless, it will continue to dominate the solar panel industry.

That said, First Solar offers a compelling proposition as its panels do not rely on polysilicon. As shown in the graphics above, China dominates polysilicon production, but for FSLR that’s not as big of a direct threat. That said, the Chinese government provides generous support to the Chinese solar industry, investing $130 billion USD in 2023, ultimately delivering cost advantages (which arguably may be unfair). The emerging glut of solar panels coming out of China has driven prices downward, with the IEA reporting that spot prices for solar panels in 2023 dropped by nearly 50%.

Crucially, many Chinese solar companies are heavily indebted, and the price wars in China and international markets should continue to add pressure on Chinese solar companies. At least 50 Chinese solar companies at various stages of the supply chain filed for bankruptcy in 2025. For example, according to Seeking Alpha's data, JinkoSolar’s (NYSE:JKS) current liabilities at nearly $7 billion, and total liabilities reach nearly $12.5 billion, a hefty amount for a company producing $11.4 billion in TTM revenues, down from $16.7 billion as of reporting in December 2023.

Chinese companies are moving to improve their situation, with industry players looking to set up a $7 billion fund to reduce polysilicon production by a third in an effort to prop up prices. Many Chinese solar companies have signed a “self-discipline” agreement, but it remains to be seen if such efforts will ultimately pay off. Difficulties in China, however, are good news for First Solar as it doesn't rely on Chinese inputs.

Putting America First With FirstSolar Solar Production

First Solar has been moving to ramp up domestic production facilities, launching plants in Alabama, Louisiana, and elsewhere. Former President Joe Biden championed the solar industry and provided extensive incentives to expand capacity. President Donald Trump isn’t likely to champion solar production specifically, but he has been working to revive America’s manufacturing sector as a whole.

First Solar’s CEO Mark R. Widmar believes that President Trump’s Big Beautiful Bill is going to be a major benefit for First Solar by keeping certain incentives in place, while also restricting access to Section 45X to companies relying on material assistance or products manufactured by foreign entities, including Chinese solar companies. Essentially, a loophole is being closed that would have benefited Chinese companies. Since First Solar doesn’t rely as extensively on Chinese inputs, they are better positioned in this current market.

Antidumping policies should also keep Chinese solar companies at bay, at least as far as the American market is concerned. The Department of Commerce has launched investigations into the Chinese solar industry, including efforts to circumvent market-protection policies through transhipment. The Big Beautiful Bill will phase out some renewable energy-specific incentives, however. Projects started by July 4th 2026 will still be eligible, which may lead to an increase in orders to get ahead of the deadline.

All in all, I am not as confident as First Solar’s CEO regarding the Big Beautiful Bill. As for the Trump administration, that too is a mixed bag. Efforts to spur American manufacturing and protect American markets from dumping should benefit First Solar. Deemphasizing solar may create headwinds, however. In the long run, I think there’s a fair chance that the Trump administration will warm up to solar, not in regards to renewable energy, but instead as a national security concern and a way to boost American manufacturing. This is speculation, however.

FSLR Fundamentals and Valuation

First Solar’s GAAP PE (TTM) is an attractive 15.79, versus a sector median of 28.1, and the non-GAAP PE measures about the same, versus a sector median of 22. The forward GAAP PE comes in at 12.2 versus a sector median of 30. Of course, PE ratios don’t tell us everything, but in an era of historically high PE ratios, I do find low PEs especially attractive. As for valuation, I turn to Alpha Sprea’s DCF calculator, which shows FSLR overvalued with modest assumptions.

I set the net income margin at 15% and the revenue growth rate at 10% to align with FSLR’s 5-year averages, according to Seeking Alpha’s data. During the last reporting, FSLR actually enjoyed a ~29% net income margin and ~15.4% revenue growth. I like to make conservative estimates; however, to try to get roughly an idea of where I think the short-term floor could be. As we can see, even with this conservative model, FSLR is undervalued by 14%. I set the discount rate at 10%, as I use that for all companies to make them easier to compare. The terminal growth rate is set at 4%, which aligns with the usually observed global GDP growth.

As for the balance sheet, FSLR has $1.153 billion cash and short-term investments. Cash and STI have been declining, which I presume is impacted by First Solar’s expansion of production facilities. Total assets, including plants and equipment, come in at ~$12.86 billion. Some plants are seeing production slashed in Vietnam and Malaysia, and I do worry they may not be as valuable now, amid the trade war, as they were last year. First Solar has a backlog worth 64.0 GW, which extends through 2030, which I find particularly appealing. Total liabilities are moderate at $4.311 billion.

It’s always nice when there’s unanimous agreement, and both my fellow Seeking Alpha analysts and the quant tools agree that FSLR is a buy right now. The quant grades, shown below, give FSLR high marks except for profitability and a mediocre revision grade. The low profitability marks draw my eye given that FSLR’s net income margin is quite healthy. Unfortunately, levered free cash flow growth is in the red by more than 32 percent, and the TTM return on asset turnover gets a D mark as well. Given that FSLR has been in a growth stage, expanding domestic and international operations, I’m not too worried about these factors, for now.

Why I Could Be Wrong

President Trump and the Republican Party are highly skeptical of renewable energies. While I believe ultimately that pursuing solar and especially a strong domestic solar industry that isn’t reliant on Chinese inputs, will put America first, those in Washington may disagree. The trade war may also cause disruptions, including retaliatory tariffs. Ultimately, First Solar will go as the domestic solar market goes, and any headwinds, political or otherwise, could weigh the company down. Further, while China's solar industry is suffering turmoil, I suspect in the long run the industry will emerge stronger as the price war cools off and subpar companies go under.

Key Takeaway: First Solar is a Buy

I believe solar energy can play a vital role in terms of reviving American manufacturing, improving and hardening the energy grid, and even for national defense. First Solar’s favorable valuation makes it attractive. That said, China’s dominance in the solar industry will create headwinds abroad, and domestic policy choices could disfavor First Solar, so there are risks investors should be aware of. Still, I think this is a moderate-risk investment that is justified by substantial upside, and thus FSLR is a buy.

data.title: High-Yield Closed-End Funds (Part 3): EHI
data.description:
Co-authored by Strategic Yield.

We continue to look at high-yield closed-end funds and get to know them in more detail. Today, we will examine Western Asset Global High Income Fund Inc. (NYSE:EHI).

Fund Overview

EHI was launched on 07/28/2003. At the end of the second quarter of 2025, the fund had total assets of around $ 289.77 million and total net assets of $205 million. The market capitalization of the fund is close to $200 million. The number of holdings is 403. The average maturity of the fund is 8.88 years. With an effective duration is 4.98 years, EHI is not overly sensitive.

The top three biggest investments in the portfolio are from Aercap Ireland CAP (1.25%), Venture Global LNG (1.23%), and First Quantum Minerals (1.12%). This suggests that the fund is betting on high-yielding but also high-risk sectors - aviation, energy, and mining.

The total return on NAV for three years is 4.56%, and for ten years it is 3.98%.

This CEF's distribution rate on NAV is 12.33%. EHI is paying its shareholders a $0.84 yearly dividend in the form of $0.0700 monthly distributions.

At the current moment, the market price of EHI is $6.60, with a NAV of $6.81 and a positive one-year Z-score of 0.53 and a positive 3-year Z-score of 0.44. It's currently trading at a 3.08% discount. It has a total leverage of 29.24%, an expense ratio of 4.14%, and a non-leverage expense ratio of 1.49%. This means that there are significant operating and leverage costs that must be accounted for by the investor.

And here is the pricing history for the last month:

The credit quality score of the fund is around a BB rating.

And here is the maturity breakdown, as we said above, it has an average maturity of 8.88 years:

EHI vs HYG

Here are the comparisons between EHI and iShares iBoxx $ High Yield Corporate Bond ETF (HYG):

If we look at the 5-year total return comparison:

We can see that HYG outperforms EHI, but if we look at the:

3-year total return comparison:

Or 6-month total return comparison:

We see that they have gone hand in hand for a long time, and now we can see that there's a divergence, and EHI outperforms HYG.

Even if we leverage HYG, the 10-year total return still will be lower than the 10-year total return of EHI:

Conclusion

Despite its high expenses and lower credit rating, EHI is an interesting choice for investors seeking a stable monthly income with a conscious risk. Trading at a discount to NAV offers additional value, and the current yield above 12% is rare in the market. If interest rates remain stable or decline, the fund price could recover, and the yield could remain competitive. However, in an adverse economic scenario, credit risks could have a significant impact on the portfolio. EHI is not for everyone. It is an income-oriented fund with medium to high risk, suitable for investors with an appetite for high yield and a willingness to accept volatility and credit risk. It is suitable as part of a diversified portfolio, but not as a core position.

data.title: Chart Of The Day: Is The PPI Proof Of An Inflation Problem?
data.description:
By Mike Larson

Everything was coming up roses on Wall Street and in Washington. Until Thursday morning at 8:30 am Eastern, that is. That’s when the Producer Price Index (PPI) inflation data dropped for the month of July.

Unlike earlier retail inflation figures, these wholesale inflation numbers were downright ugly. The headline reading rose 0.9% month-over-month, more than 4X the increase economists were expecting. The year-over-year inflation rate surged to 3.3% from 2.4% in June, as you can see in the MoneyShow Chart of the Day here.

(Source: Bureau of Labor Statistics)

What about the “core” number that excludes food and energy? It also jumped 0.9%, pushing core PPI up to 3.7% YOY. Other measures like “services less trade, transportation, and warehousing” or “goods less food and energy” all moved higher.

What does it all mean?

First, Wall Street will pay even closer attention to what Federal Reserve Chairman Jay Powell says next week at the Fed’s annual gathering in Jackson Hole, Wyoming. He’ll have a chance to “frame” the interest rate debate ahead of the Fed’s Sept. 16/17 policy meeting.

Second, markets still think the most likely outcome at that meeting is a 25-basis point rate cut. That would lower the federal funds rate to a range of 4-4.25%.

Third, Wall Street would love nothing more than for another pair of cuts to follow in October and December. Expectations for just such a scenario helped drive stocks higher earlier this week, including a huge two-day move in smaller-capitalization names.

But if this is the start of a series of reports suggesting greater pass-through effects from higher tariffs, that’s going to look more and more fanciful. And that could give both stock and bond markets agita. Keep that in mind with stocks trading in rarefied air.

Originally published on MoneyShow.com

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Ardelyx: Improved Outlook For Ibsrela And Xphozah
data.description:
Shares of Ardelyx (NASDAQ:ARDX) have been struggling in the last 12 months and the stock is down 1% since my July 2024 initiation article in which I highlighted the uncertainty around Xphozah as a potential overhang on the share price, and that the stock looks attractive based on the long-term outlook of Ibsrela in the IBS-C market.

In the meantime, Ibsrela has largely maintained its strong growth trajectory and the company raised the full-year net sales guidance range after the product’s strong performance in the second quarter, and Xphozah’s trajectory looks encouraging after the loss of access for Medicare patients at the start of the year.

The jury is still out on Xphozah and its potential, but it looks increasingly likely that there is some value in the product even without Medicare coverage.

In addition to my bullish long-term rating, I added Ardelyx to Growth Stock Forum's Momentum Ideas list after the second quarter results due to the improving growth outlook for both brands and the share price breaking the year-long downtrend line.

Xphozah doing well so far in 2025 (considering the circumstances)

Xphozah’s net sales grew 7% sequentially to $25 million, and 27% sequentially when adjusted for the release of the onetime reserve of $3.8 million in the first quarter.

As a brief reminder, Xphozah was supposed to be bundled by the CMS along with other phosphate binders at the start of 2025, but Ardelyx decided not to file for its inclusion in the bundle because it determined that Medicare coverage would be too restrictive and “effectively eliminate access to all patients.” Instead of accepting the bundling of Xphozah, the company decided to entirely focus on the commercial market, and this means it will not be reimbursed for the Medicare population which accounted for approximately 55-60% of Xphozah sales last year.

Looking at the Q4 2024 net sales of $57.2 million, a drop to just shy of $20 million in Q1 2025 (adjusted from $23.4 million for the one-time reserve release of $3.8 million that positively impacted reported sales) and $25 million in Q2 looks very reasonable considering it being locked out of the Medicare market and the uncertainty over its coverage even on the commercial side.

Management seems pleased with the progress and confident in Xphozah’s outlook as it has reiterated expectations for $750 million in peak U.S. sales.

I am thrilled to see these sales levels as I was not at all sure that the product would have any kind of traction after the loss of Medicare coverage. However, I believe we need to see a few more quarters of sales before having a better idea of Xphozah’s potential.

It is also worth noting that there is still a chance of Ardelyx prevailing in court. The company partnered with the American Association of Kidney Patients and the National Minority Quality Forum to sue CMS before the U.S. District Court for the District of Columbia, claiming that the bundling of Xphozah and other oral-only drugs was contradictory to CMS’ own regulations. Their emergency motions were denied last year, and the parties have filed an appeal with oral arguments scheduled for late September.

I am no legal expert, but it appears that the chances of Ardelyx prevailing in this case are slim. However, an unlikely win here would bring back the significant Medicare market for Xphozah and should bring a resumption of the strong growth trends that we saw in 2024.

Ibsrela’s strong second quarter results prompted an increase of the full-year guidance range

Ibsrela net sales grew 84% Y/Y to $65 million, recovering from the seemingly weak first quarter that saw sales decline almost $10 million sequentially. The first quarter is typically the seasonally weakest of the year, but that did not help improve the poor investor sentiment and there might have been doubts about the full-year net sales outlook at the time.

Those doubts were cleared after the second quarter results, and the demand for Ibsrela was high enough for Ardelyx to increase the full-year net sales guidance range from $240-250 million to $250-260 million.

Should Ibsrela net sales come within the increased guidance range, it should close the year with quarterly net sales of close to or slightly above $80 million, and that would be a pretty strong foundation for 2026.

That said, the outlook has not sufficiently changed to warrant changes in my expectations – I continue to expect annual net sales of $600-700 million by 2028, which provides the basis for my valuation range of $8.6-11.3 per share on Ibsrela that I shared in last year’s article, and which remains unchanged today.

Risks

My underlying expectations last year were that Xphozah was unlikely to regain Medicare coverage for 2025 and beyond. As such, I did not consider that a risk for the investment thesis, and this is the reality now.

The main risk, as far as my view of the company is concerned since I initiated coverage last year, was and remains Ibsrela’s performance. Its strong performance to date may provide a floor for the valuation as well as medium- and long-term upside for the stock, but we still need to see continued strong sales performance if the stock is to continue trending higher.

Ardelyx has a good balance sheet with $238.5 million in cash and equivalents and $200 million in long-term debt. The cash position was strengthened in Q2 through a $50 million credit tranche from SLR Capital with options for additional $100 million and two $50 million tranches through 2026. Cash burn from operations should decline in the following quarters (it was around $38 million and $25 million in Q1 and Q2, respectively), and the company may start to use the cash to start building a pipeline.

Global and industry-specific macro risks apply, though Ardelyx has no meaningful ex-U.S. presence and this reduces the risk of Most Favored Nation pricing of Ibsrela and Xphozah.

Conclusion

The year-to-date performance of Ibsrela and Xphozah put Ardelyx back on the growth path. The next two quarters will have tougher year-over-year comparisons since Xphozah’s net sales were north of $50 million in the third and fourth quarter of 2024, but from there, we should see steady year-over-year growth with the company approaching profitability in the second half of 2026.

data.title: Swiss Re: Strong Results And Buyback Potential Keep Us Positive
data.description:
We are resuming our coverage of Swiss RE AG (OTCPK:SSREF, OTCPK:SSREY) in light of its first-half 2025 results. Our audience is aware of our expertise in the insurance sector, and in H1 2025, we already published quarterly analyses of the leading integrated insurers — Zurich, AXA, and Allianz. As for Swiss Re, it ranks among the world’s largest reinsurers and is headquartered in Zurich. Since our initiation of coverage (early January 2025), we are up by more than 33% (including the dividend already paid - Fig. 1).

We based our buy recommendation on 1) the virtuous relationship between the insurance and reinsurance sectors, 2) conservatism on the combined ratio, and 3) CHF remuneration. Regarding the shareholders' remuneration, the company increased its DPS by 8%.

Fig 1

Q2 Results and Our Positive Take

Starting the company's bottom line, Swiss RE delivered a Q2 net profit of $1.32 billion, up 11% compared to last year and ahead of Wall Street consensus expectation. Analysts were projecting a net profit average of $1.19 billion. In H1, Swiss RE total earnings reached $2.6 billion with a ROE of 23.0% (Fig. 2).

By segment, in H1, Property & Casualty Reinsurance's top-line sales reached $8.9 billion, and with a combined ratio below 80%, Swiss Re delivered a net profit of $1.2 billion. The combined ratio was mainly driven by lower Nat Cat ($214 million) and a higher discount rate. The Corporate Solutions segment reached a net profit of $430 million with a combined ratio of 88.2%, while the Life & Health Reinsurance net profit stood at $839 million. The former segment recorded stable sales in H1 2025, but gross insurance sales increased by 5% on a quarterly basis. The combined ratio reached 88.0%. At the same time, the latter segment reported a CSM balance release of $410 million, primarily driven by FX change. Going to the balance sheet, Swiss Re's solvency ratio reached 264%.

Source: Swiss Re Q2 results ppt - Fig 2

Why are we still positive?

  1. Starting with Swiss Re’s balance sheet, its strength is nothing short of formidable. The solvency ratio reached a level much higher than the regulatory requirements (180%), and the Swiss Re target range was set between 200%-250% (Fig. 3). This already included the dividend, which Swiss Re paid in mid-April 2025. Swiss Re’s capital strength did not stem from disposals or one-off capital releases, but from organic generation supported by robust earnings. As a result, alongside our expectation of a rising dividend per share yearly, we now also foresee the possibility of a share buyback announcement during Q3 results or in conjunction with the FY 2025 results. In our estimates, we anticipate at least a share repurchase of $1 billion;
  2. Referring to Figure 2, Swiss Re has maintained a steady performance quarter after quarter. In our previous update, we raised our net income forecast to a full-year target of $4.6 billion; in the first half alone, the company already delivered $2.6 billion. Based on Swiss Re’s recent disclosure, it is worth noting that "natural catastrophe activity is typically higher in the second half of the year." Still, the pruning actions in the Swiss Re segment increased our confidence that the company will likely outperform the <85% combined ratio guidance;
  3. Still related to the business, in H1, Swiss Re's top-line insurance sales (premium) decreased by -5.9% with a material 8% reduction in the P&C division (Fig. 4). The company mentioned increased seasonality in H1 compared to H2. This is something that we do not record in our initial analysis of Swiss Re's historical P&L. Despite the decline in premiums, the company delivered record results. This was also supported by a recurring income yield of 4.1% (10 basis points up by H1 2024 - Fig. 5).

Fig 3

Fig 4

Fig 5

Earnings Changes and Valuation

Swiss Re did not change its Fiscal Year 2025 net income targets of >$4.4 billion. Forecasting a reinsurance company’s financials presents unique challenges, but we expected an upward guidance revision to reflect improved performance. In our last update, we had already increased our net profit projection to $4.6 billion. It is worth noting that our forecasts were already above the Wall Street consensus, and the current positive price reaction (+3%) indicates that the market had been pricing in more modest results. At this stage, we maintain this earnings projection, deriving an earnings per share of CHF 15.38. There is also no change to the current outstanding shares, even if we expect a buyback. With a 12x P/E target, which is well aligned with our insurance peer coverage, the Swiss Re share price reached CHF 184.5 per share ($57.25 in ADR). This implied a 21% upside.

Risks

Downside risks include: 1) high claims inflation with Natural Catastrophes, cybersecurity & terrorist attacks, 2) accounting changes and higher regulatory requirements (Swiss Re is very much protected with a solvency ratio of 264%), 3) lower reinvestment yield, 4) change in financial markets, the company is mainly exposed to credit risks, but it also holds equity type investments, 5) lower shareholders' remuneration, and 6) higher tax rate.

Conclusion

Swiss Re delivered strong results in H1 2025. The company reported solid results in the Solvency ratio, underlying margin, and profits. Given an ROE above 20%, we are still not pricing in additional upside, such as the buyback or a higher P/E target. So, we remain buyers.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Bankers Expect An Upswing In Deposits Over The Next 12 Months - S&P Survey
data.description:
The majority of US bankers are bullish on deposit growth over the next year, according to S&P Global Market Intelligence's second quarter 2025 US Bank Outlook Survey. While macroeconomic uncertainties linger as a result of President Donald Trump's tariff policies, the positive sentiment reflects the banking industry's favorable outlook on net interest income as deposit costs continue to drop.

The Take

➤ An overwhelming majority of 81% forecast deposit growth over the next 12 months.

➤ An increased proportion of respondents expect small business and wholesale or brokered deposits to contract over the next year.

➤ Deposit competition is expected to rise in their respective market areas over the next six months.

Bright outlook on deposit growth

S&P Global Market Intelligence surveyed US banking industry professionals between June 3 and July 1 and found that 81.1% of the respondents expect deposits to grow at their institutions over the next 12 months, up from 76.1% in the first quarter 2025 survey and the highest mark since the fourth quarter of 2022 survey.

The US banking industry recorded total deposit growth of greater than 1% on both an aggregate and median basis in the first quarter of 2025, surpassing loan growth, with the increase largely attributed to demand deposits.

Deposit composition outlook

The proportion of respondents expecting a drop in small business deposits at their institution over the next year increased sequentially by 6.1 percentage points to 19.8%. Similarly, survey participants expecting a decline in wholesale or brokered deposits rose 2.3 percentage points on a sequential basis to 15.1%.

The US banking industry scaled down brokered deposits in the first quarter of 2025 as US banks gained inflows of more attractive deposit categories. Reliance on overall wholesale funding increased sequentially, although it remained at the low end of the range over the last two years.

Interest rate outlook

The median expected interest rate on interest-bearing transaction accounts at the end of 2025 is 0.75%, while the median expected rate for savings accounts, including money market deposit accounts, is 1.75%.

Additionally, the median expected rate for a 1-year certificate of deposit at the end of 2025 is 3.25%.

Many US banks started aggressively trimming down deposit rates in the first quarter of 2025, following the Federal Reserve's 100 basis point interest rates reduction in 2024.

Funding

Among the respondents, 42.2% said that their organization had increased its use of deposit rate specials over the last 12 months, the highest percentage in any of the 10 options given for the question on liquidity and deposit sources. Meanwhile, 35.3% also projected that their institution would increase its usage of deposit rate specials over the next 12 months, which was also the highest percentage among all options given.

Just shy of 25% of respondents stated that their institution had increased its usage of advances from the Federal Home Loan Bank system over the last 12 months, and 28.4% of respondents expected their institution would expand usage of FHLB advances over the next 12 months.

Deposit competition expected to heat up

Among those surveyed, 49.5% disclosed that competition for deposits in their market area increased over the past 12 months. Meanwhile, 50.5% of the respondents anticipate a heightened deposit competition in their area over the next six months, while 42.9% expect it to remain the same.

According to Stephens analyst Terry McEvoy, some banks may face increased deposit competition as a result of the Federal Reserve lifting the $1.95 trillion asset cap imposed on Wells Fargo & Co. (WFC).

About this analysis

S&P Global Market Intelligence surveyed 106 US financial institution clients on various topics including expected deposit growth. Of the 106 participants, 74 worked for commercial banks or thrifts, 25 for credit unions, six for other US institutions and one chose not to answer the question.

The online survey was conducted between June 3 and July 1, 2025.

The margin of error for topline statistics is +/- 9 points at the 95% confidence level.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Trump's Cannabis Reforms Could Hand Altria A Fortune
data.description:
Back in March, I laid out a detailed thesis for why I believed that Altria was positioned to profit from U.S. cannabis reform and turn it into a long-term engine for continued growth. Trump's recent comments made on August 11, stating that cannabis rescheduling is "under review" with a decision coming "in the next few weeks" has turned my early speculation into a time-bound possibility.

During a recent press briefing at the White House, Trump stated:

“We’re looking at reclassification, and we’ll make a determination over, I would say, the next few weeks. That determination, hopefully, will be the right one.”

Trump also noted that cannabis reclassification was a "very complicated subject," acknowledging that it is complex and implied it will require deliberation.

I believe that the logic I laid out in my previous article for the administration to reclassify cannabis from a Schedule I to Schedule III drug will only strengthen over the coming months. This in turn could allow Altria to transition from a passive cannabis stakeholder to a market leader in the U.S.

Before recapping the core reasons why I believe that cannabis reclassification will move ahead, I'd like to point out that Trump has a track record of setting near-term policy clocks and acting on them regularly. Trump did this with the Abraham Accords (2020), the USMCA Trade Deal (2018-2020), and most recently with his Tariff Announcements (2025). Generally, if Trump puts the clock on a decision, it's because the groundwork is almost done and that the outcome is leaning towards what he's signaling politically, which in this case is cannabis reclassification.

It would also make little sense for any leader to give such a specific timeline only to walk it back. If the Trump administration really had no interest in reclassification, the smart move would be to say nothing at all. Drawing attention to denying reclassification would waste a valuable bargaining chip as well as hand his opponents a talking point that he's out of touch with public opinion, especially when 70% of Americans already support outright cannabis legalization.

Another core factor at the play is that reclassification can be carried out without Congress needing to pass new legislation, as rescheduling is primarily an executive branch process. Reclassifying also ties in with economic logic: The U.S. cannabis market is already projected to reach $45-58 billion by 2028, and reclassifying would remove the 280E tax burden, boosting net margins for companies by 20-30% and making the industry more attractive and profitable for companies to enter and operate in.

On balance on probabilities, the economic, political and executive factors now tilt towards a reclassification of cannabis relative to earlier this year. With this aspect of the thesis appearing probable, the next is examining how and why I believe Altria is uniquely positioned to capitalize on this shift, despite there being minimal tangible signals that the company is making overt moves. My view is that if reclassification takes place, Altria will play some cards it has been holding quietly, and will act both quickly and decisively. I am buying more shares of Altria in preparation of this upside catalyst, one which could the most defining and important strategic pivot in its modern history. Although still highly speculative, this catalyst gives me confidence in reiterating my Strong Buy rating for Altria.

Why Altria’s Cannabis Position Is Stronger Than It Looks

The Trump administration rescheduling cannabis from Schedule I to Schedule III would likely act as Altria's market-entry catalyst, but here's what the actually means and what it would allow the company to achieve under these new terms.

Rescheduling opens the prescription & medical cannabis pathway on a federal level. This doesn't mean that all cannabis products automatically become legal to prescribe, but it does mean they can be legally researched, approved, and prescribed under federal law (similar to how substances like anabolic steroids or ketamine are treated). This means that Altria could develop cannabis inhalers, vaporizers and other products that pass FDA approval and be sold in nationwide pharmacies, as opposed to only in certain state dispensaries.

Altria holds multiple vaporizer patents, including a vaporizer explicitly designed for THC or CBD. It also holds a patent for a heat-not-burn device capable of decarboxylating cannabis compounds. These patents show a commitment to early-stage R&D into the industry. The company also has over 40 patents for devices described broadly as being able to "vaporize" materials, which includes, but is not limited to, cannabis formats.

In the past, the company has been involved in cannabis lobbying activities at both the federal and state levels. Altria spokesman George Parman explicitly backed U.S. cannabis legalization "under an appropriate regulatory framework".

So while we don't know the specific form Altria's cannabis entry will take, here's what we can infer with high probability: Altria is highly interested in the cannabis market and has been building the options to enter it for years. To handwave the patent activity, Cronos acquisition, lobbying, and explicit public comments inverts the burden of proof. It would require believing that the company did all of these things coincidentally and without any definable reason if not to enter. The more probable explanation is that these were all deliberate steps toward a entry plan that has been waiting for a green light. Altria's silence about its cannabis position is both predictable and legally prudent. If Altria were to spell out its market intentions before reclassification even takes place, that would be like Ford announcing intentions to rollout street-legal flying cars while lawmarkers still classify them as illegal aircraft. It would be premature, risky, strategically self-defeating, and pointless.

While full-stack entry is on the cards, Altria could also choose lower-exposure routes, such as through licencing its device IP, sell non-plant-touching hardware into the channel, take minority stakes or convertibles in companies, or wait longer until federal risk drops even more. It's too soon to speculate which specific entry avenue Altria will pursue, but directionally it seems likely it will lean on its vaporizer patents since those are its existing assets it has invested in heavily already.

From Oral Nicotine Beat to Cannabis Upside

Altria's most recent quarterly result showed promise that it's able to execute and scale beyond its smokable products segment. Adjusted EPS beat expectations ($1.44 vs ~$1.39 expected) and revenue rose 0.2% year-over-year to $5.29 billion. It's on! oral tobacco shipment volumes jumped 26-27%, which helped to offset the structural decline in its tobacco business.

Here's a profit model for how cannabis would layer on top of its existing business using some conservative assumptions and priors. It should be noted that this cannabis model shows incremental, and not transformative earnings potential. However, a sustained valuation uplift for a single category for a company like Altria can lead to second-order effects such as multiple expansions or higher re-ratings for the entire business, which is where I think its future fortunes will come from, especially as the market matures.

Altria currently trades for $65.50, with a market cap of $110~ billion and a forward P/E of 11.9. An additional $100 million of after-tax profit would mean that's worth $1.19 billion in market value at current multiples, or around $0.70 per share.

If we take the U.S projections for the cannabis retail market, which sits at $46~ billion on the low end for 2028, the math shows a straightforward upside. The formula I'm using is: profit ≈ TAM × Altria’s effective market share × net margin.

If Altria were to enter the cannabis market in a full-stack way, meaning owning brands or joint ventures that touch the plant, then even a 1% share at 15% net margins would lead to $69 million in annual profits. When applying the current market multiples, that's around $821 million in added value or $0.49 per share. When applying a less conservative assumption, such as a 2% share at an 18% margin, that figure climbs to roughly $166 million or a $1.17 share price uplift.

Below are some scenarios and implied per-share upside depending on what lever Altria decides to pull to enter the market.

Scenario Profit (NOPAT) Implied Value @ 11.9×

Per-Share Upside (+)
Hardware/licensing $22 M ~$262 M $0.16
Full-stack (1% share @ 15% margin) $69 M ~$821 M $0.49
Full-stack (2% share @ 18% margin) $166 M ~$1.97 B $1.17

Risks

While the Trump administration could make a decision on rescheduling cannabis within the next few weeks, finalizing that rescheduling could take months or even years. This could delay the thesis from playing out and make it difficult for Altria to quickly capture a meaningful market share.

The upside from cannabis is also tiny in comparison to Altria's $8.76 billions in earnings base, so I'm prepared to say that it's still possible that the firm will ignore its optionality in cannabis entirely. I do believe that this risk is slim given its previous moves in the industry, but it will depend on the managerial will inside the organization and how they weigh up the risks of entering a new market against its declining tobacco business and the threats of pivoting to a whole new type of industry.

Takeaway

With cannabis rescheduling on the cards for Altria, its optionality to enter this market is underappreciated. I believe that a decisive move into the market is probable that it would add a new high-growth and high-margin operating segment to the company's portfolio. It would help it to complete its broader objective of diversifying from its tobacco core and may also trigger a re-rating for parts of its business, increasing valuation further.

I therefore rate Altria as a Strong Buy and I am adding to my position in preparation for this catalyst to play out.
